title: 👑 Elite Challenge: Relative Clauses & Comparatives Under Pressure

questions.prompt: A student writes: "The museum, that we visited last summer, was built in 1890." What is wrong with this sentence?

options.text: Nothing is wrong; 'that' is always interchangeable with 'which'.
'that' cannot be used in a non-defining relative clause; it must be replaced with 'which'.
The commas are wrong; remove them and the sentence is correct.
'that' should be replaced with 'who' because museums are important places.

questions.explanation: Non-defining relative clauses (set off by commas) add extra, non-essential information. They require 'which' for things — never 'that'. 'That' is only used in defining relative clauses, where no comma is used. Removing the commas (option C) would make it defining, which changes the meaning entirely.

questions.prompt: Combine these two sentences into ONE using the correct relative pronoun: "We read a report. Its conclusions surprised the entire committee."

options.text: We read a report which conclusions surprised the entire committee.
We read a report that its conclusions surprised the entire committee.
We read a report whose conclusions surprised the entire committee.
We read a report where its conclusions surprised the entire committee.

questions.explanation: 'Whose' replaces a possessive ('its conclusions' = 'the report's conclusions'). It can refer to both people AND things in formal English. 'Which conclusions' is ungrammatical without a preposition; 'that its' repeats the pronoun and is incorrect; 'where' is for places, not possessive relationships.

questions.prompt: Choose the sentence in which the relative pronoun CAN be omitted without making the sentence ungrammatical:

options.text: The teacher whose class I attended retired last week.
The city where I was born is far from here.
The novel that she recommended to me was excellent.
The student who answered first won the prize.

questions.explanation: A relative pronoun can be omitted only when it is the OBJECT of the relative clause. In option C, 'that' is the object ('she recommended [it] to me'), so it can be dropped: 'The novel she recommended to me was excellent.' In option D, 'who' is the SUBJECT ('who answered first'), so it cannot be omitted. 'Whose' and 'where' can never be omitted.

questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the 'the … the …' double comparative structure?

options.text: The more you practice, the more better your English becomes.
The harder you study, the best results you will get.
The harder you study, the better your results will be.
More harder you study, better your results will be.

questions.explanation: The 'the + comparative … the + comparative' structure requires a comparative form (not a superlative) in BOTH clauses. 'The harder … the better' is correct. Option A wrongly doubles 'more' and 'better'; option B uses the superlative 'best' in the second clause; option D omits the definite article 'the' and adds a redundant 'more' before 'harder'.

questions.prompt: Complete the sentence with the correct form: "Among all the students in the school, Yasmine scored ___ marks on the national mock exam." (few)

options.text: the fewer
the fewest
the least few
fewer than

questions.explanation: When comparing three or more (here: all students in the school), we use the superlative. 'Few' forms its superlative as 'the fewest' (regular short-adjective pattern: add -est). 'The fewer' is the comparative, used only for two items. 'The least few' is not a real form. 'Fewer than' is a comparative requiring a second element to compare with.

questions.prompt: A student writes: "My handwriting is not as worse as my brother's." Identify the error and choose the corrected version.

options.text: My handwriting is not as bad as my brother's.
My handwriting is not as worst as my brother's.
My handwriting is not more bad than my brother's.
My handwriting is not as badly as my brother's.

questions.explanation: The 'as … as' structure always requires the BASE (positive) form of the adjective — never the comparative or superlative. The base form of 'worse' is 'bad', so the correct sentence is 'not as bad as'. Using 'worse' inside 'as…as' is the most common trap with irregular adjectives. 'Badly' is an adverb and cannot describe handwriting as a noun adjunct here.
